evidence.quote: Obviously, we talk about ImageNet also just SAIL at Stanford is where a lot of the work happened, Google Cloud, which a lot of the breakthroughs happened.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: So when I, for example, came to Stanford, in the world of academia, I was very close to this thing called tenure, which is have the job forever at Princeton.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But I chose to come to Stanford because... I love Princeton.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: It's just at that moment there are people who are so amazing at Stanford and the Silicon Valley ecosystem was so amazing that I was okay to take a risk of restarting my tenure clock.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I could see the stress. And so I think that advice is really important. Just like what will actually make you feel fulfilled in what you're doing, not just where's the fastest growing company, where's the... Who's going to win? I don't know. I want to make sure I ask you about the work you're doing today at Stanford, at the HCI. I think it's the-
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And I felt that Stanford, one of the world's top university in the heart of Silicon Valley that gave birth to important companies from NVIDIA to Google, should be a thought leader to create this human-centered AI framework and to actually embody that in our research education and policy and ecosystem work.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: It involves hundreds of faculty across all eight schools at Stanford, from medicine to education, to sustainability to business, to engineering, to humanities to law.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 视觉智能 (visual intelligence)
entities.how_described: 本集提到李飞飞选择通过视觉智能的视角来看待人工智能,因为人类是深深依赖视觉的动物,大量的智能建立在视觉感知之上。

evidence.quote: I chose to look at artificial intelligence through the lens of visual intelligence because humans are deeply visual animals.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think two things happened to myself that brought my own career so close to the birth of modern AI is that I chose to look at artificial intelligence through the lens of visual intelligence because humans are deeply visual animals.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So I choose to look at visual intelligence and my PhD and my early professor years, my students and I are very committed to a north star problem, which is solving the problem of object recognition because it's a building block for the perceptual world, right?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Of course, I come from the world of visual intelligence and I was just thinking there's so much we can push forward beyond language because humans, humans use our sense of spatial intelligence, a world understanding to do so many things and they are beyond language.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And in the meantime, I was doing a lot of robotics research and it dawned on me that the linchpin of connecting the additional intelligence, in addition to language embodied AI, which are robotics, connecting visual intelligence, is the sense of spatial intelligence about understanding the world.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: AI 寒冬 (AI winter)
entities.how_described: 本集把它描述为公众和投资者对 AI 几乎失去信心、资金枯竭的时期(80年代末到21世纪初),没多少人关注,连课程都很难。

evidence.quote: The work you did actually was the spark that brought us out of AI winter.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And then there's this what people refer to as the AI winter where people just gave up almost, most people did, and just, okay, this idea isn't going anywhere.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And then the work you did actually was essentially the spark that brought us out of AI winter and is directly responsible for the world where now of just AI is all we talk about.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: It was still smack in the middle of the so-called AI winter, meaning the public didn't look at this too much.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 主观能动性 (agency)
entities.how_described: 本集在结尾谈及 AI 对人类的影响时提到,强调没有任何技术应该夺走人类尊严和人自主做决定与行动的主观能动性。

evidence.quote: One of my other favorite takeaways from what you just shared is just such an example of high agency and just doing things that's kind of a meme on Twitter.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: But no technology should take away human dignity and the human dignity and agency should be at the heart of the development, the deployment, as well as the governance of every technology.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 强化学习 (reinforcement learning)
entities.how_described: 本集在解释“苦涩的教训”时顺带提及,指出提出该观点的 Richard Sutton 教授做了很多强化学习研究。

evidence.quote: Well, first of all, there are many bitter lessons, but the bitter lessons everybody refers to is a paper written by Richard Sutton who won the Turing Award recently, and he does a lot of reinforcement learning.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 深度学习 (deep learning)
entities.how_described: 本集说 2012 年 ImageNet 挑战赛上的突破,被许多人认为是深度学习时代的开始(层数很多的神经网络),正在加速自动驾驶等系统的“大脑”。

evidence.quote: We continue to do our own research, but 2012 was the moment that many people think was the beginning of the deep learning or birth of modern AI because a group of Toronto researchers led by Professor Geoff Hinton, participated in ImageNet Challenge, used ImageNet big data and two GPUs from NVIDIA and created successfully the first neural network algorithm that can...
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So the journey is going to be, there's many aspects, elements, and of course one could say, well, the self-driving car, early algorithm were pre deep learning era.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So deep learning is accelerating the brains.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: DARPA
entities.how_described: 本集提到李飞飞的同事 Sebastian Thrun 带领斯坦福的车赢得了第一届 DARPA 自动驾驶挑战赛。

evidence.quote: If you look at the history of self-driving car, my colleague Sebastian Thrun took Stanford's car to win the first DARPA challenge in 2006 or 2005.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1
